Article.ArticleTitle: Positive membrane curvature induced by a CFEM-domain protein is essential for fungal invasion of the host.

Abstract.AbstractText: The germination of asexual spores is one of the critical steps in fungal infection of the host. Extensive research has been conducted on the mechanisms by which pathogenic fungi infect their hosts, leading to theoretical insights into the roles of hydrolases, turgor pressure, mechanical force, and sinusoidal tip growth. However, no prior studies have linked proteins involved in membrane curvature to fungal infection mechanisms. This study reveals that the CFEM-domain protein CFEM7 induces positive curvature of the cytoplasmic membrane in the entomopathgenic fungus Beauveria bassiana, promoting the bending of fungal germ tubes and facilitating penetration of the insect cuticle. Our results demonstrate that the absence of BbCFEM7 significantly reduces curvature of germ tubes and germination rate of conidia, impairing the fungus's ability to penetrate and infect the insect host. The curvature of the germ tubes of the ΔBbCFEM7 mutant strain was significantly lower than that of the WT and ΔBbCFEM7::BbCFEM7 replacement strains. Molecular dynamics simulations and biophysical experiments confirm that BbCFEM7 directly integrates into the phospholipid bilayer, inducing positive membrane curvature. Further experiments involving truncated BbCFEM7 expressed in the ΔBbCFEM7 strain indicate that the amino acid residues 1-194 of BbCFEM7 constitute the critical region responsible for curvature induction. Moreover, the 1-194 AA section significantly restored the conidial germination rates and the curvature of the germ tubes. This discovery provides a novel perspective on the mechanism of fungal infection and offers potential targets for developing new biological control strategies. This study establishes a direct relationship between membrane curvature-inducing proteins and fungal pathogenicity.
